questions.questionText: The most common cause of myocardial infarction is:
questions.optionB: Atherosclerotic plaque rupture with superimposed thrombosis
questions.explanation: MI: most commonly due to rupture of an unstable atherosclerotic plaque (thin fibrous cap, large lipid core, inflammatory cells) → exposure of thrombogenic material → platelet aggregation + coagulation cascade → coronary artery thrombosis → myocardial ischemic necrosis. LAD: most commonly affected (anterior wall MI). Time course: 4-12h → coagulative necrosis, 1-3d → neutrophil infiltration, 3-7d → macrophage cleanup (maximum softening — risk of rupture), 2-8 weeks → granulation tissue, months → scar. Reference: Robbins — MI.

questions.optionB: SLE (sterile vegetations on BOTH surfaces of valve leaflets)
questions.explanation: Libman-Sacks endocarditis: nonbacterial thrombotic (sterile) vegetations found on BOTH surfaces of mitral (most commonly) and aortic valve leaflets in SLE. Also associated with antiphospholipid syndrome. Unlike rheumatic verrucae (verrucous, on line of closure only), or infective endocarditis (large, irregular, destructive). Marantic endocarditis: sterile vegetations in debilitated/cancer patients. Reference: Robbins — Libman-Sacks.

questions.optionB: Large and medium-sized elastic and muscular arteries
questions.explanation: Atherosclerosis: chronic inflammatory disease of large/medium arteries (aorta, coronary, carotid, cerebral, renal, peripheral). Pathogenesis: endothelial injury → lipid accumulation (LDL oxidation) → inflammatory response (macrophages → foam cells) → smooth muscle proliferation → fibrofatty plaque → complicated plaque (calcification, rupture, thrombosis). Risk factors: dyslipidemia, HTN, diabetes, smoking, age, family history. Most common cause of death worldwide (IHD, stroke). Reference: Robbins — Atherosclerosis.

Aschoff bodies: foci of fibrinoid necrosis surrounded by inflammatory cells including Anitschkow cells (caterpillar/owl-eye cells — activated macrophages) and Aschoff giant cells. Pathognomonic of rheumatic carditis/rheumatic heart disease. Rheumatic fever: post-GAS (Group A Streptococcal) infection → molecular mimicry → cross-reactive antibodies damage heart valves (mitral > aortic). Jones criteria for diagnosis. McCallum patch: left atrial endocardial thickening. Reference: Robbins — Rheumatic Heart Disease.

questions.questionText: The most common valvular lesion in rheumatic heart disease is:
questions.optionB: Mitral stenosis (fish-mouth appearance of mitral valve)
questions.explanation: Rheumatic heart disease: mitral valve most commonly affected. Chronic: mitral stenosis (thickened, fibrosed, calcified, fused commissures → fish-mouth/buttonhole orifice). Can cause left atrial dilation → atrial fibrillation → stasis → thrombus → systemic embolism. Order of valve involvement: mitral > aortic > mitral+aortic > tricuspid (rarely pulmonary). Reference: Robbins — RHD Valvular.

questions.optionB: Four-chamber dilation with systolic dysfunction (decreased EF)
questions.explanation: DCM: most common cardiomyopathy. Features: biventricular dilation → systolic dysfunction (decreased EF <40%) → congestive heart failure. Causes: idiopathic (most common), genetic (titin, lamin mutations), alcohol, doxorubicin (adriamycin), viral myocarditis (Coxsackie B), peripartum, Chagas disease. Gross: heavy, globular heart with mural thrombi. Hypertrophic CM (HCM): diastolic dysfunction, asymmetric septal hypertrophy, LVOT obstruction. Restrictive: infiltrative (amyloid). Reference: Robbins — DCM.

questions.questionText: Vegetation in infective endocarditis caused by Staphylococcus aureus is typically:
questions.optionB: Large, bulky, destructive, and with high virulence (can affect normal valves)
questions.explanation: S. aureus: most common cause of ACUTE infective endocarditis (aggressive). Can attack NORMAL valves. Large, bulky, friable vegetations → valve destruction (ring abscesses), septic emboli (brain, kidneys, spleen), high mortality. Subacute IE: S. viridans (most common on previously damaged valves, e.g., RHD, mitral valve prolapse) — smaller vegetations, indolent course. IV drug users: S. aureus, tricuspid valve. HACEK organisms: subacute IE in culture-negative cases. Duke criteria for diagnosis. Reference: Robbins — IE.

questions.optionB: Rapid fluid accumulation in the pericardial space → compression of the heart → decreased filling → cardiogenic shock
questions.explanation: Cardiac tamponade: pericardial fluid (blood, effusion, pus) accumulates rapidly → compresses heart → impaired diastolic filling → decreased cardiac output → cardiogenic shock. Beck's triad: hypotension, distended neck veins (jugular venous distension), muffled/distant heart sounds. Pulsus paradoxus: exaggerated BP drop (>10 mmHg) during inspiration. Causes: trauma (hemopericardium), post-MI (ventricular free wall rupture), malignancy, infection (viral/TB). Treatment: emergent pericardiocentesis. Reference: Robbins — Tamponade.

questions.optionB: Hyaline arteriosclerosis (benign HTN) and hyperplastic arteriosclerosis ('onion-skin' — malignant HTN)
questions.explanation: Arteriosclerosis types: (1) Hyaline: homogeneous, pink, glassy thickening of arteriolar walls (benign nephrosclerosis in HTN, diabetes). (2) Hyperplastic: concentric laminated 'onion-skin' thickening of arterioles with fibrinoid necrosis (malignant/accelerated HTN, necrotizing arteriolitis). (3) Mönckeberg medial calcific sclerosis: calcification of media of muscular arteries (elderly, does NOT narrow lumen, clinically insignificant). Reference: Robbins — Arteriosclerosis.

questions.questionText: The most common primary cardiac tumor in adults is:
questions.optionB: Myxoma (left atrial myxoma is most common)
questions.explanation: Myxoma: most common primary cardiac tumor in adults. Usually left atrium (attached to interatrial septum near fossa ovalis). Pedunculated, gelatinous, may cause ball-valve obstruction of mitral valve (intermittent symptoms with positional change), systemic embolization, constitutional symptoms. Histology: stellate/spindle cells in myxoid matrix. Can be part of Carney complex (familial myxomas, skin pigmentation, endocrine overactivity). In children: rhabdomyoma (associated with tuberous sclerosis). Metastatic tumors to heart are far more common than primary. Reference: Robbins — Cardiac Tumors.
